Summary: This study aimed to develop and validate a radiomics strategy using machine learning classifiers from conventional imaging and ADC to differentiate recurrent GBM from RN after CCRT or radiotherapy. The diffusion radiomics model showed robustness with an AUC of 0.80 in the independent test set.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, leading to a representative study sample.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on histopathology or clinicoradiological follow-up, which are acceptable reference standards for this type of study.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from two institutions, indicating a multi-center approach.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source includes conventional MRI sequences and ADC, which are part of standard glioma protocols.

Item6.reason: The study provides details of the MRI acquisition protocols in the supplementary material.

Item7.reason: The study includes patients with new or enlarging contrast enhancement within the radiation field 12 weeks after CCRT or RT, which is an appropriate interval to validate the presence or absence of target conditions.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of the contrast-enhancing solid portion of the tumor.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed semi-automatically with interactive level-set region of interest, implying human intervention.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the use of interactive level-set region of interest and edge-based and threshold-based algorithms.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation was performed through a consensus discussion of two neuroradiologists, so it was not produced by a single reader or automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features.

Item11.reason: The study describes the preprocessing techniques, including N4 bias correction and signal intensity normalization.

Item12.reason: The study used PyRadiomics, which is a standardized software, and mentions the version number.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types and provides information on the feature extraction parameters.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline.

Item14.reason: The study does not explicitly mention the removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study uses LASSO and MI for feature selection, which can be considered as methods for removing redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions stratified ten-fold cross-validation, which implies a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study mentions clinical characteristics but does not explicitly discuss the handling of confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study reports AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, which are appropriate performance evaluation metrics for the task.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention calibration assessment.

Item23.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the performance of uni-parametric imaging with multi-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study does not compare the radiomics approach with a non-radiomic method representative of clinical practice.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the model with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The study tests the model on an independent data set from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study includes external testing with data from another institution.
